title: Swift Concurrency Part 1: Actors & Isolation
subtitle: This is Part 1 of a 3-part series on Swift Concurrency, Mainly focused on Actors & Isolation

body.title: How to follow along
body.text: Every code block in this article is a building block. By the end, you'll have a complete working playground you can paste directly into Xcode and run. No inputs needed.

body.title: The Problem Actors Solve
body.text: Picture two threads reaching for the same variable at the same moment. One reads while the other writes. The result is an undefined [b]data race[/b]. Before Swift's actor model, you defended against this manually with DispatchQueue, [tag]NSLock[/tag], or [tag]os_unfair_lock[/tag]. Forget one lock and your app crashes in production, on a device you can't reproduce.
Actors are Swift's built-in answer. The compiler enforces thread safety for you. no locks, no queues, no guessing.

body.title: Step 1: Preparations & Extras
body.text: This example follows a simple bank account transaction model. Before we introduce the actor, we define a basic error type to handle cases such as attempting to withdraw more than the available balance. This is a plain [tag]enum[/tag] and is not related to concurrency yet.
Clean and descriptive. When a withdrawal fails, the error carries exactly what was requested and what was actually available.

body.title: Step 2: Defining an Actor
body.text: An [tag]actor[/tag] looks almost identical to a [tag]class[/tag]. The difference is what the compiler does with it: [b]only one piece of code can access its mutable state at a time.[/b] No locks required, the Swift runtime serialises access automatically.

Notice [tag]transfer(amount:to:)[/tag] calls [tag]other.deposit(amount:)[/tag] with [tag]await[/tag]. Even though we're inside an actor method, since [tag]other[/tag] is a differentactor instance crossing its boundary still requires [tag]await[/tag].

body.title: Step 3: Actor Isolation in Action
body.text: This is where actors feel different from classes. Try accessing [tag]balance[/tag] without [tag]await[/tag] and the compiler won't let you:
The compiler is protecting you. [tag]balance[/tag] is isolated to the actor to read it from outside, you must cross the boundary with [tag]await[/tag].

body.title: Step 4: nonisolated Opting Out for Safe Members
body.text: Not everything inside an actor needs protection. [tag]accountNumber[/tag] and [tag]owner[/tag] are [tag]let[/tag] constants, they never change, so there's no possible data race. Forcing callers to [tag]await[/tag] them is unnecessary overhead.</br>The [tag]nonisolated[/tag] keyword opts a specific member out of the actor's isolation:
Now [tag]CustomStringConvertible[/tag] conformance works synchronously, no [tag]await[/tag] needed:

body.code:
let account = BankAccount(accountNumber: "001", owner: "Alice", initialBalance: 1000)
print(account.description)  // ✅ No await. nonisolated, reads only constants

body.text: The compiler enforces the rule: if you try to read [tag]balance[/tag] inside a [tag]nonisolated[/tag] member, it's a compile error.

body.title: Step 5: Actor Reentrancy (The One Gotcha)
body.text:
Actors are reentrant. When an actor method hits an [tag]await[/tag], it suspends and the actor becomes free to handle other calls. When it resumes, state may have changed.
Here's why this matters for our bank:
The fix is to re-check state after any [tag]await[/tag], or as we've done in [tag]transfer(amount:to:)[/tag] perform all local mutations before the [tag]await[/tag]:

body.title: Step 6: @MainActor: The UI Actor
body.text:
[tag]@MainActor[/tag] is a built-in global actor that represents the main thread. Any property or method marked [tag]@MainActor[/tag] is guaranteed to run on the main thread, which is exactly what SwiftUI and UIKit require.
In a playground we don't have a UI, but the concept is identical:
In a real app, your [tag]ObservableObject[/tag] view models are typically marked [tag]@MainActor[/tag] so every property update is automatically safe for SwiftUI:

body.title: The Complete Playground
body.text: Here is everything assembled into a single file you can paste into an Xcode Playground and run immediately. It creates two accounts, runs a series of operations concurrently, and prints the results.

body.code:
Accounts created:
  Account 001 (Alice)
  Account 002 (Bob)
Initial balances:
  Alice: $1000.0
  Bob:   $500.0
After Alice deposits $200:
  Alice: $1200.0
After Bob withdraws $100:
  Bob: $400.0
After Alice transfers $300 to Bob:
  Alice: $900.0
  Bob:   $700.0
Attempting overdraft ($9999 from Bob)...
  ❌ Failed: requested $9999.0, only $700.0 available
Running 5 concurrent $10 deposits into Alice's account...
  Deposit 1 completed
  Deposit 3 completed
  Deposit 2 completed     ← order may vary: they run concurrently
  Deposit 5 completed
  Deposit 4 completed
  Alice final balance: $950.0

body.text: The concurrent deposit section is the most instructive part. Five tasks all hit the actor simultaneously but the actor serialises them, so [tag]balance[/tag] is updated atomically each time. Without an actor, this would be a data race. With one, the compiler guarantees it's safe.

rows: A reference type whose mutable state is serialised by the runtime
Required to cross the isolation boundary from outside
Opts a member out of isolation for `let` constants or stateless members
State can change across an `await` mutate locally before suspending
The global actor for the main thread; used on view models and UI code
